coredata.dc:title: On the intriguing emission characteristics of size tunable carbon dots derived from functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes
coredata.dc:creator: Saheeda, P.
Sabira, K.
Joseph, Julin
Jayaleksmi, S.
coredata.dc:description: Aqueous dispersions of carbon dots (C-dots) showing size tunable multi-colours in day light are obtained through surface functionalization of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The size of the C-dots varies with the functionalization temperature and smaller C-dots are found to be formed, at higher temperatures. The average size of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C lies in the range 6 nm–18 nm, based on transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The C-dots synthesized at these temperatures exhibit blue, green and golden yellow colours in day light without being excited by any external source. The structural characterizations using X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy confirm the presence of a high density of functional groups on the surfaces of the C-dots, giving rise to high aqueous solubility. Compared to the Raman spectrum of the MWCNTs, the spectrum of the C-dots shows a significant increase in the intensity of the defect related D band with respect to that of the crystalline G-band, which indicates the presence of a high concentration of defects and the formation of smaller sp2 carbon clusters. The synthesized C-dots exhibit size dependent optical absorption and emission characteristics. The intriguing optical properties of these C-dots, which include the multi-colour emission at day light and the excitation wavelength and the pH dependent photoluminescence emission, can be mainly attributed to the formation of sp2 clusters of different sizes embedded in the sp3 carbon matrix.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Functionalization
Multi-walled carbon nanotubes
Photoluminescence
De-protonation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of C-dots at different magnifications, synthesised at temperatures of 100 °C (a–c), 80 °C (d–f) and 60 °C (g–i).
Aqueous dispersions of the C-dots synthesised at (a)100 °C, (b) 80 °C and (c) 60 °C.
XRD plots of (a) pristine MWCNTs and (b) C-dots.
FTIR spectra of (a) MWCNTs and (b) C-dots.
Raman spectra of MWCNTs and the synthesized C-dots.
UV-Visible absorption spectra of the C-dots synthesized at different temperatures.
Relationship between the band gap energy and the size of the C-dots.
PL emission spectra of the C-dots synthesized at different temperatures.
PL spectra of the C-dots with increasingly longer excitation wavelengths.
PL intensity variations of the aqueous solutions of the C-dots in different pH environments from 1 to 11.
Schematic representation of the sp2 clusters of different sizes, embedded in the sp3 matrix of the C-dots.

head.ce:title: On the intriguing emission characteristics of size tunable carbon dots derived from functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division for Research in Advanced Materials, Department of Physics, Cochin University of Science and Technology, Cochin, Kerala, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Division for Research in Advanced Materials
Cochin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology (NITC), Calicut, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aqueous dispersions of carbon dots (C-dots) showing size tunable multi-colours in day light are obtained through surface functionalization of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). The size of the C-dots varies with the functionalization temperature and smaller C-dots are found to be formed, at higher temperatures. The average size of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C lies in the range 6 nm–18 nm, based on transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images. The C-dots synthesized at these temperatures exhibit blue, green and golden yellow colours in day light without being excited by any external source. The structural characterizations using X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy confirm the presence of a high density of functional groups on the surfaces of the C-dots, giving rise to high aqueous solubility. Compared to the Raman spectrum of the MWCNTs, the spectrum of the C-dots shows a significant increase in the intensity of the defect related D band with respect to that of the crystalline G-band, which indicates the presence of a high concentration of defects and the formation of smaller sp2 carbon clusters. The synthesized C-dots exhibit size dependent optical absorption and emission characteristics. The intriguing optical properties of these C-dots, which include the multi-colour emission at day light and the excitation wavelength and the pH dependent photoluminescence emission, can be mainly attributed to the formation of sp2 clusters of different sizes embedded in the sp3 carbon matrix.

Aqueous dispersions of the C-dots synthesized at (a) 100 °C, (b) 80 °C and (c) 60 °C.
Image

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon dots (C-dots) endowed with intriguing optical and electronic properties continue to garner the research attention of scientific community globally because of their multitude of applications in diverse areas including optoelectronic devices, sensors, bioimaging and in drug delivery [1–6]. The fluorescent nanoparticles derived from carbon nanotubes, graphene and other carbonaceous materials are quite attractive in respect of their benign nature and they form a class of potentially non-toxic and biocompatible fluorescent nanomaterials with high aqueous solubility and stable chemical properties [5,7–9]. The tunable emission characteristics, good photo-stability and resistance to photobleaching are some of the advantageous attributes of the C-dots compared to conventional fluorescent dyes [10,11].

The C-dots can be visualized as made up of spherical graphitic fragments of carbon atoms with size less than 10 nm with relatively high oxygen content [12]. These oxygen functionalities on the surfaces of the C-dots facilitate the tailoring of their surface properties for applications in energy storage, energy harvesting and solid-state lighting systems [13–16]. The C-dots decorated hybrids show great potential as excellent electrode materials for highly efficient hybrid super capacitors with high specific capacitance and exceptional cycling stability because of their fast charge transportation and ionic motion during the charge– discharge process [17]. Recently, C-dots based light-emitting devices are emerging with unprecedented application prospects in lighting technology in respect of their low cost, stable and bright light emission and eco-friendly nature [13]. The solubility in polar solvents and the broad absorption characteristics ranging from visible to near infrared (NIR) wavelength regions are quite appealing for the applications of C-dots as photovoltaic sensitizers [18].

Fluorescent C-dots as electron donors or acceptors present a versatile platform for realizing highly sensitive chemical and biological sensors [19,20]. The fluorescence sensing offers fast, sensitive, selective and reliable detection of metal ions and biomolecules based on the fluorescence quenching of the C-dots in the presence of the analyte (quencher) [21,22]. The development of C-dots with intense fluorescence, good aqueous solubility and low toxicity, has revolutionized the current fluorescent labeling technology in the biomedical field [4,23]. The size smaller than biological cells and the compatibility with the biological media facilitate unparalleled interactions of the C-dots with the bio-molecules [24]. Though, semiconductor quantum dots have already created a niche as fluorescent probes in bio-imaging, the presence of heavy metal elements such as cadmium and lead in them generates long-term toxicity issues which severely limit their use in in vivo diagnostics and staining [25,26]. At this juncture, the C-dots with their bright, stable and tunable photoluminescence emission can be considered as green substitutes to the existing semiconductor quantum dots, offering great prospects in imaging and diagnostics.

Carbon nanotubes are one dimensional allotropes of carbon, envisioned as rolled sheets of graphene with their ends capped with half of fullerene like molecules. Generally there are two main kinds of nanotubes like single-walled nanotubes (SWNTs), with diameter 1–2 nm, consisting of a single graphene layer and multi-walled nanotubes (MWNTs), consisting of several concentric graphene cylinders, where, weak van der Waals forces bind the tubes together [27]. The unusual properties of CNTs like high aspect ratio, exceptional strength and stiffness, extraordinary thermal conductivity and excellent mechanical and electrical properties offer immense research opportunities in almost all fields of science and technology [28,29]. In spite of these captivating attributes, CNTs are inherently insoluble in almost all aqueous and organic solvents, which is the main limitation in tapping their potentials. It has been reported that surface functionalization of CNTs can effectively improve their solubility and reactivity and render biocompatibility and low toxicity and these aspects help to extend their potentials in biomedical areas [30]. Several methods have been developed for the surface modification of CNTs based on covalent and non-covalent modifications. The covalent functionalization through chemical modifications greatly enhances the dispersion of CNTs in aqueous media which opens possibilities to integrate other functional groups on to their surfaces [31].

Out of different “Top down” strategies developed for the synthesis of colloidal carbon nanostructures, chemical functionalization using strong acids forms the most widely adopted and cheap method. Chemical oxidation of CNTs in concentrated acid mixtures of H2SO4/HNO3 at high temperatures, generally results in the cutting down of CNTs into nanoscale C-dots whose size and morphology can be controlled effectively through optimization of reaction parameters such as time and temperature [32]. In addition, this process produces high density oxygen containing functional groups on the C-dot surfaces, which enhances their solubility and biocompatibility profile so that they can be easily integrated into other molecules and biological systems [33]. These C-dots have exciting optical properties different from their carbon precursors, which open doors for various chemical and biological applications.

The most fascinating feature associated with C-dots is the observation of visible fluorescence emission. Recently, lots of studies have been conducted to explain the mechanism behind the origin of the visible luminescence from oxidized CNTs. Though different explanations have been put forward, the real mechanism behind this exciting phenomenon still remains unclear and has yet to be disclosed. It is reported that the defect states introduced by the functionalization of CNTs seem to be primarily responsible for the luminescence in the visible range, because of the significant changes occurring in the electronic states near the Fermi level induced by the oxidizing process [34]. The studies conducted by Qian and co-workers hold the view that, visible fluorescence originates from carbon quantum dots produced by the oxidation of MWCNTs, and can be attributed to the presence of sp2 carbon clusters isolated by sp3 carbons during oxidation rather than defect sites [35]. According to Lin's group, the visible emission from CNTs can be ascribed to the trapping of excitation energy by defect sites in the nanotube structure, which are passivated upon functionalization of the nanotubes [36]. Ding and co-workers have reported on the role of surface states in controlling the luminescence properties of the hydrothermally synthesized C-dots of different sizes which exhibit bright and stable luminescence from blue to red regions under a single-wavelength UV excitation [37]. Peng's group has described the synthesis of luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs), of different sizes simply by choosing different reaction temperatures. The size-dependent luminescence observed from the synthesized C-dots has been attributed to the emissions from the zigzag edges of the sp2 structures [32]. In most of the previous reports obtained so far, the visible luminescence is observed when C-dots are excited at shorter wavelengths, usually under UV excitation [38]. Though there are many reports on the observation of visible emission from carbon nanostructures, the temperature dependent, size tunable synthesis of C-dots showing multi-colour emission in day light is being reported for the first time through this article.

The most interesting aspect of the present work is the size dependent, multi colour emissions observed from the C-dots in day light without being excited by any external source. The size of the C-dots is found to vary with the functionalization temperature and smaller C-dots are formed at higher reaction temperatures. Corresponding to the functionalization temperature of 100 °C, 80oCand 60 °C, C-dots of average size in the range of 6 to 18 nm are formed with blue, green and golden yellow emissions respectively, which can be seen with naked eyes, without being excited by any external excitation source. This intriguing phenomenon of multiple colour emissions of the C-dots in day light is observed for an optimized concentration of the aqueous solutions of the C-dots. Upon photo-excitation, these C-dots exhibit quite interesting photoluminescence emission features which include, emission characteristics dependent on the wavelength of the excitation source and the pH of the medium, which are of application prospects in various imaging fields.

Multi-walled Carbon Nanotubes (MWCNTs) with 95% purity and an average diameter 40 nm and length of a few microns were obtained from Graphene Super market, USA. Nitric acid (HNO3, purity > 70%) and sulfuric acid (H2SO4, purity > 98%), and NaOH were obtained from Merck Life Science Pvt. Ltd, and were used as received. All chemicals used were of analytical grade. Deionized water was used for washing throughout the experiments.

For effecting the functionalization, MWCNTs (25 mg) were initially stirred in hydrochloric acid for 24 hours and then washed in deionized water several times to remove any metal impurities and dried overnight at 70 °C. The purified MWCNTs (10 mg) were then functionalized by refluxing in acid solution containing concentrated H2SO4/HNO3 in 3:1 ratio at different temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C for 4 hours [34,39]. After cooling to room temperature, deionized water was added very slowly to the C-dots solution to observe the colour fluorescence.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM-) images of the C-dots were obtained on JEOL model JEM- 2100 having a resolution of 0.24 nm and an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The X-ray diffraction study of the C-dots sample was done using the Rigaku X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα (λ = 1.5481Ao) radiation operating at 30 kV and 20 mA at a scan speed of 2° per minute. The FTIR spectroscopic studies of the samples were done to confirm the presence of functional groups on the surfaces of the C-dots, using SHIMADZU FTIR Spectrophotometer operating in the range 400–4000 cm−1. The Raman spectra were recorded on a Labram HR (Jobin-Yvon, Horiba Group, France) microspectrometer with a 532 nm laser as the source. The optical absorption spectra of the C-dots were obtained using the JASCO-V570 spectrophotometer in the wavelength range 200 nm to 800 nm and the photoluminescence emission (PL) spectra, using the Fluoromax- 3 spectrofluorometer.

Surface modification of CNTs through chemical functionalization is a powerful method to tune the surface properties of carbon nanotubes for tailor made applications and to enhance the solubility and biocompatibility. Acidic oxidation brings about drastic changes in the size and shape of CNTs depending on the functionalization temperature. During functionalization, fragmentation takes place initially at the cap positions of nanotubes where the strain is more due to the pentagonal arrangement of carbon atoms. This is followed by fragmentation of side walls [27]. As the reaction proceeds, dots like carbon nanostructures termed as C-dots are formed. The TEM images of the C-dots at different magnifications synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C are shown in Fig. 1 (a-i). The C-dots obtained at 100 °C consist of nearly spherical particles of average size about 6 nm, as shown in Fig. 1(a,b,c). Images at higher magnifications show that they are not single entities but are constituted by five or more C-dots stuck together and appear like clusters of irregular shapes as portrayed in the figure. A much closer observation shows that the surfaces of these nanostructures are not uniform as a result of the formation of a large number of defects on their surfaces. Since acidic oxidation at elevated temperatures is highly corrosive, which causes severe etching of the CNTs, it is difficult to control the size distribution and morphology of the fragments, which results in the formation of C-dots of irregular size and shape [27,40].

The TEM images of C-dots obtained at 80 °C shown in Fig. 1(d,e,f) are of spherical morphology with average size around 12 nm. At still lower temperature of 60 °C, the TEM images illustrate, as shown in Fig. 1(g,h,i) shapeless nanotube fragments distributed in the aqueous medium and they have an average size of about 18 nm. It can be seen that C-dots of smaller size are obtained for higher reaction temperatures. The C-dots so obtained are highly water soluble and form a homogeneous and stable dispersion. The aqueous dispersions of the C-dots obtained at these temperatures exhibit blue, green and golden yellow colour in day light as can be seen from Fig. 2 , which agrees well with the size distribution of the C-dots obtained using TEM technique.

The XRD patterns of pristine MWCNTs and the C-dots, are shown in Fig. 3 . The pristine MWCNTs exhibit a narrow intense peak at 26°, which can be indexed as the (002) reflection of graphite with interlayer spacing of 0.34 nm [41]. On the other hand, the C-dots show a broader, less intense peak centered around 19°. The shift in the peak position as well as the broadening of the peak observed in the XRD pattern of the C-dots can be attributed to the strain introduced in the nanotube structure due to the attachment of oxygen functional groups, produced during acidic functionalization [42]. Since the XRD line width is inversely proportional to the size of the C-dots, broadened peak results from C-dots of smaller size, as evident from the figure.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopic (FTIR) studies were carried out to ascertain the formation of oxygen functional groups on the surfaces of the C-dots as a result of the chemical functionalization of the MWCNTs. The FTIR spectrum of the C-dots shown in Fig. 4 (b), shows a broad peak at 3450 cm−1, attributed to the –OH stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups. The broadness of this peak is a clear indication of the presence of a large number of residual –OH groups. The intense sharp peak observed in the spectrum of the C-dots at 1649 cm−1 can be assigned to the C=O stretching mode of the –COOH groups [43,44]. This peak is more pronounced, signifying the presence of a high density of carboxyl groups, attached to the defective sites of the C-dots through covalent bonding. The peak at 1355 cm−1 can be indexed to C–H deformation vibrations and that at 1205 cm−1 to alkoxy C–O–C stretching vibrations. The band around 1050 cm−1 is assigned to the (C-O) stretching vibrations of the ether group [45]. The relative increase in the intensity of the –OH and the C=O vibrational peaks is a measure of the degree of oxidation. These oxygen functionalities on the surfaces of the C-dots make them more hydrophilic and enhance their solubility and the biocompatibility profile. For a comparison, the FTIR spectrum of pristine MWCNTs is also shown in the figure.

Raman spectroscopy is a widely used non-destructive tool in the structural characterization of carbon nanostructures. The Raman spectrum of pure graphite shows a single sharp band in the range 1550 cm−1 to 1600 cm−1, referred to as the graphitic (G) band. The presence of defects in the pure sample gives rise to a second band denoted as D band in the range 1300–1350 cm−1, which can be attributed to the presence of the disordered sp3 carbon atoms [33–35]. The Raman spectra of the pristine MWCNTs and the C-dots synthesized at 100 °C are shown in Fig. 5 and both the spectra contain these two signature bands. The G band of MWCNTs is sharp and centered around 1581 cm−1. This band arises from the tangential mode of vibration of the sp2 hybridized carbon atoms and the D band at 1354 cm−1 is due to the defects within the graphitic structure. The Raman spectrum of the C-dots is quite similar to that of the pristine MWCNTs, but the bands get broadened and shifted to 1540 cm−1 and 1312 cm−1 respectively which clearly reveals that the C-dots are comprised of small sp2 clusters embedded in a disordered sp3 carbon matrix. The highly ordered crystalline sp2 carbon network becomes more amorphous which is also evident from the intensity ratio (ID/IG), the ratio of the intensity of the disorder mode to that of the graphitic mode [35]. The ID/IG of MWCNTs is 0.758 and that of C-dots is 1.165. The significant increase in the ID/IG of the C-dots is an indication of the presence of the high degree of structural defects due to functionalization and the formation of C-dots of much smaller sp2 clusters [36]. The particle-size can be calculated from the ID to IG ratio, using the Tuinstra–Koenig (TK) relation, (ID/IG) = C(λ)/La, where La is the cluster size and C(λ), an empirical constant that depends on the excitation laser source. For the 532 nm laser source used in the present study, the value of C (λ) is 4.96 nm [37]. The particle size of the C-dots obtained from the Raman spectral analysis is about 5.78 nm; which is in agreement with the average particle size of the C-dots obtained from the TEM analysis.

The size dependent optical absorption of the C-dots samples synthesized at different temperatures was studied using UV-Vis absorption spectroscopy technique. The UV−visible absorption spectra of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C show absorption peaks at 288 nm, 306 nm and 322 nm respectively with an absorption tail extending to the visible region, as shown in Fig. 6 . These peaks observed in the UV range correspond to the π−π* transitions involving aromatic sp2-hybridized carbon atoms [46]. The n-states of the C-dots can be attributed to the carbonyl functional groups containing lone pair of electrons and when the functional groups carrying lone pairs are bonded to aromatic sp2-hybridized carbons, electron transitions from the n-states of the functional groups to the π* states of the aromatic carbons can happen, giving rise to absorption peaks at higher wavelengths. The peaks observed above 310 nm thus correspond to the n-π* transitions [47]. It can be seen from the figure that the absorption peaks shift towards shorter wavelengths with the increase of the functionalization temperature, which indicates that the reaction temperature can affect the absorption properties of the C-dots. It has been reported that surface functional groups attached to the aromatic carbons play a major role in determining the light absorption properties of the C-dots. The smaller C--dots obtained at higher functionalization temperatures are endowed with more number of defect states and correspondingly the optical absorption takes place at shorter wavelengths [38].

The band gap energy of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C are 3.26 eV, 2.80 eV and 2.62 eV respectively. The band gap energy depends on the size of the C-dots so that the band gap decreases with the increase of the size of the C-dots, which is the generally observed trend [48]. The band gap values obtained in the present case agree with the above findings, where the blue emitting C-dots of the smallest size have the highest band gap. This indicates that the band gap energy can be tuned by tuning the size of the C-dots which again depends on the synthesis parameters like functionalization temperature. The relationship between the band gap energy and the size of the synthesized C-dots is shown in Fig. 7 . As evident from the figure, the band gap energy decreases from 3.26 eV to 2.6 2eV as the reaction temperature is varied from 100 °C to 60 °C. The energy gap of the C-dots mainly originates from the different surface states formed during the formation of the C-dots of different sizes. Since the luminescent centers on the surfaces of the C-dots are carbon atoms and bonded oxygen atoms, the band gap of such a structure between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) strongly depends on the attached oxygen functionalities. A higher oxidation temperature implies the formation of smaller C-dots and correspondingly the band gap gets enhanced [38].

Photoluminescence emission is one of the most fascinating features of carbon nanostructures. The photoluminescence emission spectra of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C are shown in Fig. 8 . The three samples show emission peaks at 435 nm, 470 nm and 498 nm respectively corresponding to excitation at 340 nm. It can be seen from the figure that, as the temperature of functionalization increases, the PL intensity increases and the PL peak position exhibits blue shift [49]. The C-dots synthesized at the highest temperature of 100 °C show the highest PL intensity, having the smallest size with their PL emission in the blue region. Lower synthesis temperatures result in the increase of size, and correspondingly the emission wavelengths get red shifted as evident from the figure. In addition, the C-dots show broad PL emission spectra covering most of the visible region. Different emissive sites as well as defect sites of different sizes present on the surfaces of the C-dots might be contributing towards the broad PL spectra [50]. Although the chemical and electronic structures of the C-dots vary depending on the synthesis methods, the C-dots exhibit PL emission mainly in the blue region. Different routes have been suggested for the possible mechanisms of PL emission in C-dots including quantum confinement effects, the presence of emissive traps, oxygen-containing groups and free zigzag sites. However, the exact mechanism is yet to be established [51]. In the case of the C-dots synthesized through acidic oxidation from the MWCNTs, the sp2 clusters of different sizes and the defect states on the surfaces of the C-dots are found to play a dominant role in the PL emission. The acidic oxidation facilitates the generation of more defective sites and sp2 clusters of different sizes on the surfaces of the C-dots, which endow the C-dots with lots of new energy levels.

The C-dots exhibit excitation wavelength dependent PL emission, which is a unique feature shown by all types of C-dots. The multi-colour PL emission allows the selection of different emission colours under different excitation wavelengths which extends the scope for the applicability of the C-dots as fluorescent tags in cell labeling [52]. In the present work, the C-dots synthesized at 80 °C were studied for the excitation dependent PL emission. The PL emission spectra corresponding to excitation from 360 nm to 460 nm, in 20 nm increments, are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen from the figure that as the excitation wavelength increase, the PL emission intensity decreases and the PL emission wavelength shifts towards higher wavelengths. In the present work, for the increment in excitation wavelength from 360 nm to 460 nm, the PL emission peaks get shifted from 458 nm to 520 nm. This property exhibited by the C-dots can be a consequence of the selection of the C-dots of different sizes with different emissive sites on their surfaces for optical excitation. For each excitation wavelength, some of the emissive sites would be excited, with the resulting fluorescence, showing excitation-dependent emission spectra [49].

To further analyze the PL emission properties of the C-dots in different chemical environments, the PL intensity variations of the C-dots in different pH conditions were investigated. The pH dependence was studied on the C-dots synthesized at 80 °C. The PL emission spectra of the C-dots show significant variations in the PL intensity, for pH values ranging from 1 to 11 as shown in Fig. 10 . The pH dependent PL emission results reveal that, under alkaline conditions, the PL emission has the highest intensity, whereas under strong acidic conditions as at pH 1, significant decrease in the PL intensity is observed, as evident from the figure. For in between pH values, small variations in the PL intensity are noted. These observed changes in the PL intensity are brought about by the carboxyl moieties on the surfaces of the C-dots which are protonated in acidic pH and de-protonated in basic pH environments [49]. Under acidic conditions, the carboxyl groups on the surfaces of the C-dots get protonated, forming a reversible complex between the functional groups and the protons. This will result in conformational changes in the functional groups attached to the surfaces of the C-dots which lead to inactivation of the emissive sites and the consequent decrease in the PL intensity significantly. On the other hand, the de-protonation of the carboxyl groups at higher pH levels can rebuild the PL intensity because, higher pH levels will change the distribution of the π electron cloud density between the carbon backbone and the surface states, resulting in stronger fluorescence emission [53]. However, the changes in pH levels do not have any influence on the PL emission wavelengths, and they affect only the emission intensities.

The aqueous solutions of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C exhibit blue, green, and golden yellow colour in day light at room temperature, as shown in Fig. 2. It can be seen that the reaction temperature can effectively control the size distribution and the shape of the nanostructures and correspondingly the emission wavelength of the C-dots. The different emission colours may originate from the C-dots of different size and shape, having different types of surface defects [32], as represented schematically in Fig. 11 . The strong emission in the visible range is generated from the quantum-sized graphite fragments of the C-dots, and it deeply depends on the sp2 cluster size which further depends on the HOMO–LUMO gap of the graphene fragments. As the size of the fragment increases, the gap energy decreases and for gap energy in the visible spectral range, emission happens in the visible range by absorbing appropriate wavelength from the visible light [54]. The defect states also have a significant role in deciding the emission colours, because, higher oxidation temperatures result in the creation of more defect states which may absorb radiation of appropriate wavelengths from the day light and give rise to emission in the visible region [27]. The sp2 domains of different sizes embedded in the sp3 matrix of the C-dots are responsible for the size tunable multi-colour emission, observed in day light.

Carbon dots (C-dots) of different size and morphology were synthesized through acidic oxidation of MWCNTs at different temperatures. The size of the C-dots formed is found to depend on the reaction temperatures and higher temperature treatment results in the generation of smaller C-dots. The novelty of the present work is the observation that the aqueous solutions of the C-dots synthesized at temperatures of 100 °C, 80 °C and 60 °C exhibit blue, green and golden yellow emission colours respectively at day light, without being excited by any external source. The visible colour emission shown by the C- dots in solution is quite compatible with the size distribution of the C-dots. The Raman spectral studies confirm the formation of sp2 clusters of smaller size embedded in the sp3 matrix of the C-dots. Investigations on the size dependent optical properties of these C-dots reveal that the reaction temperature has a decisive role in the optical absorption and the photoluminescence (PL) emission properties of the C-dots. The excitation dependent PL emission exhibited by the C-dots can be attributed to the different size distributions of the C-dots and the presence of various emissive sites on the surfaces of the C-dots. The C-dots exhibit significant variations in the PL intensity in different pH environments. The multi-colour emission of the C-dots at day light can be attributed to the recombination of electron–hole pairs localized within small sp2 carbon clusters of differing sizes embedded within the sp3 matrix. The excitation dependent multi-colour PL emission property exhibited by these C-dots offers application prospects of these C-dots as fluorescent probes in cell labeling.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors Saheeda.P and Sabira.K, gratefully acknowledge the University Grants Commission, Government of India for providing teacher fellowship under the Faculty Improvement Programme.
